# AI and LLM Agent Instructions for {Project Name}

This document gives LLM agents and code assistants the rules, syntax, patterns,
and known traps needed to use {Project Name} correctly on the first try.

> **Source of truth:** {the code and its godoc/docstrings, or a named spec doc
> such as SPECIFICATION.md}. This manual is *derived* from it — if they ever
> disagree, the source of truth wins.

<!--
HOW TO USE THIS TEMPLATE
- This manual is the *delta over your existing docs*, not a second copy of them.
  If the godoc / docstrings / README already cover something well, LINK to it and
  move on — don't restate it. A manual that merely duplicates good in-language
  docs goes unread (a library's agent reaches for godoc first) and wastes tokens
  if anyone forces a read.
- FRONT-LOAD the highest-value content: the traps and the one canonical recipe
  come first, because that is the delta an agent cannot get from signatures alone.
- For a LIBRARY, the agent's default path is the in-language doc surface
  (godoc / docstrings) + a runnable example — it often won't open this file on its
  own. So ALSO inline the decisive traps + canonical recipe THERE; treat this file
  as the exhaustive "read-the-manual" reference (the fallback), not the only home.
- Keep it lean and concrete: a correct copy-pasteable snippet beats prose. Delete
  sections that don't apply (don't leave empty headers). Aim for the smallest file
  that lets an agent succeed first-try — if it grows past a couple of screens you
  are probably restating docs that already exist; link out instead.
- Every claim must match the code and ground-truth spec (no drift). Ship it IN the
  repo and make sure it travels with the published package (module vendoring / npm
  "files" / Python package data / crate include). Validate it with the clean-agent
  evaluation (see GUIDELINE §3f).
-->

---

## 1. Syntax / API cheat sheet

{The minimum an agent needs to call the thing: the core entry points with exact
signatures, the primary types, and the one canonical "hello world" call. If the
godoc already lists these clearly, keep this short and link to it instead of
restating it.}

```{lang}
{smallest complete working example — imports through a successful call}
```

---

## 2. Traps (the highest-value section — put it first)

{The places an agent will otherwise guess wrong. This content does not exist in
signatures or a typical README, so it is the main reason this file earns its keep.
For each trap: the symptom, the rule, and the *why*. For a library, mirror these
few inline in the doc surface too — that's where the agent actually lands.}

- **Argument order / signature shape.** {If functions differ, give the unifying
  rule and tell the agent to verify signatures rather than copy from a sibling.}
- **Concurrency.** {What is safe to call concurrently; what must not be shared.}
- **Surprising-but-deliberate behavior.** {State it and the reasoning so the agent
  builds the right mental model and doesn't "fix" it.}
- **Build tags / feature flags / modes.** {Anything that changes behavior.}
- **Setup that must happen first.** {Encodings/handlers/registration that must
  happen before use, with the code.}

---

## 3. Recipes (the common real-world patterns)

{For each frequent pattern, a copy-pasteable, known-correct snippet. The single
most common pattern is also worth inlining in the doc surface for a library.}

### {Pattern name — e.g. "the most common record/request shape"}
```{lang}
{recipe}
```
{One line on when to use it and any caveat.}

---

## 4. Crucial rules & constraints

{Hard requirements not obvious from signatures that didn't fit as a trap. One
subsection each; keep to what the agent genuinely can't infer from the docs.}

---

## 5. Debugging & error diagnosis

{How an agent should diagnose failures. Name the inspection/trace tools and the
structured error types: which fields they expose (offset, location, field,
expected-vs-actual) and how to match on them.}

```{lang}
{example: catch the typed error, read its location/field, act on it}
```

---

## 6. Advanced (validation, performance modes, codegen, …) — link out when you can

{Remaining capabilities an agent occasionally needs. Keep each to one line + a
snippet, or just LINK to the reference/spec — this is the section most likely to
bloat into a doc duplicate. Don't restate a complete reference here.}

---

## 7. Contributor note

{If agents may modify this codebase, point to AGENTS.txt and state the single most
important invariant inline (e.g. "changes must be mirrored across N code paths —
see AGENTS.txt").}
